channel.title: Overthewire on Mohit Monnappa
channel.description: Recent content in Overthewire on Mohit Monnappa

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Level 33
item.description: All levels solved!! 🎉

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Level 32
item.description:
In Linux, using printenv will give all the environment variables. Some common ones that are good to know:

Variable Description
TERM Current terminal emulation
HOME Path to home directory of currently logged in user
LANG Current locale settings
PATH Directory list to be searched when executing commands
PWD Pathname of the current working directory
SHELL / $0 The path of the current user’s shell (most important!)
USER Currently logged-in user

Any command is converted to uppercase and executed, and there are no uppercase commands in Linux. Therefore, we need a way to start a normal terminal. $0 is the shell path — run it:

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Level 31
item.description:
Read the README.md — it says we have to push a file named key.txt with the text May I come in?. But the .gitignore file ignores .txt files, so delete the .gitignore file and add the text to key.txt, then push it to the master branch to get the password:

bash
1 2 3 4 5 6
rm .gitignore
cat > key.txt
# May I come in?
# ^C
git add .
git push origin master

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Level 30
item.description:
There is a feature called tag in git. Git tagging is a way to mark specific points in the history of the repository.

To see tags:

bash
1
git tag

To show the tag message:

bash
1
git show tag_name

Here:

bash
1 2
git tag
git show secret

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Level 29
item.description:
Clone the repo and go inside. There are multiple branches — check by:

bash
1
git branch -a

To go to a branch:

bash
1
git checkout branch_name

Check the dev branch. Once you switch to a branch the file contents will be of that branch. Check the contents of the files — here, the README file has the password:

bash
1
cat README.md

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Level 28
item.description:
Clone the repo and go inside. See the git log:

bash
1
git log

Go to a commit using:

bash
1 2
git checkout commit_hash
git show

Or in a single line:

bash
1
git show commit_hash

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Level 27
item.description: Create a temp directory and clone the repo. Include port number 2220 after localhost. The password is the same as this level’s password. Go into the folder and cat the README.

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Level 25 & 26
item.description:
This is a little tricky. First, print out the shell that bandit26 is using:

bash
1
cat /etc/passwd | grep bandit26

It says /usr/bin/showtext. Cat that file:

bash
1
cat /usr/bin/showtext

It uses the more command. Use SSH to log in to bandit26 with the SSH private key. Resize the window before running so that the more command triggers. Then:

  1. Type v to open vim
  2. Once vim is opened, you can resize the window
  3. Set the shell: :set shell=/bin/bash
  4. Open the shell: :shell

Now print the password of bandit26:

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Level 24
item.description:
We have to try all possibilities from 0000 to 9999 to get the password of the next level. Write a script that combines bandit24’s password and the code with a space in between and saves it to a file, then cat the file and pipe it to nc.

Script:

bash
1 2 3 4 5
current_pwd="gb8KRRCsshuZXI0tUuR6ypOFjiZbf3G8"
for i in {0000..9999}
do
 echo "$current_pwd $i" >> poss.txt
done

Command:

bash
1
cat possibilities.txt | nc localhost 30002

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Level 23
item.description:
The script will run as bandit24 and it executes all the files in /var/spool/bandit24/foo/ directory, then deletes them after a minute. We have to write a script to print bandit24’s password and save it to a file so that the cron job will execute it.

bash
1 2 3
mktemp -d
cd location
cat > script.sh

Script contents:

bash
1 2
#!/bin/bash
cat /etc/bandit_pass/bandit24 > password

Then:

bash
1 2 3 4 5
chmod 777 script.sh
touch password
chmod 666 password
cp script.sh /var/spool/bandit24/foo/
cat password

Wait for a minute for the password to appear.

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Level 22
item.description:
Print the shell script. Since this will run as bandit23, whoami will give bandit23 — so to get the mytarget value, copy the command and replace myname with bandit23 so it will hash it with bandit23 as the user. Then cat the output in the /tmp folder:

bash
1 2
echo I am user bandit23 | md5sum | cut -d ' ' -f 1
cat /tmp/output_of_above_command

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Level 21
item.description:
Go to the cron directory to see the cron jobs. We get the path of the script that is being executed — cat the file to see what the script is doing. It reads the contents of the passwd file and redirects it to a file in the /tmp directory. Just cat that file:

bash
1 2 3
cat /etc/cron.d/cronjob_bandit22
cat /usr/bin/cronjob_bandit22.sh
cat /tmp/whatever_file_the_above_command_gave

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Level 20
item.description:
The setuid binary makes a connection to localhost on the port you specify as a command-line argument. It then reads a line of text from the connection and compares it to the password in the previous level (bandit20). If the password is correct, it will transmit the password for the next level (bandit21).

To achieve this, set up a netcat listener to get back the password. Start a netcat server in the background using & (ampersand):

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Level 19
item.description:
The setuid binary is set to bandit20’s user. Check the file type of the given file — it says it is an executable, so try running it. The euid is set to bandit20, which means this gives the current user the privileges of the creator of the file. So just cat bandit20’s password:

bash
1
./bandit20-do cat /etc/bandit_pass/bandit20

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Level 18
item.description:
After logging in, the .bashrc file automatically logs you out instantly — so pass the command in quotes after the SSH query to execute commands before it logs you out:

bash
1
ssh -l bandit18 bandit.labs.overthewire.org -p 2220 "cat readme"

First use ls in quotes and we see readme file is present, so just cat the readme file.

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Level 17
item.description:
Use diff to compare lines of two files:

bash
1
diff --suppress-common-lines -i -y passwords.old passwords.new

-i is to ignore cases and -y is to display side by side.

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Level 16
item.description:
Run nmap to see the services on the ports. Use the version argument and specify the port numbers:

bash
1
nmap -sV -p 31000-32000

Then through ncat, use the SSL option, connect, and submit the current level’s password.

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Level 15
item.description:
Use openssl with s_client to connect to the server. Login and type:

bash
1
openssl s_client -host localhost -port 30001

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Level 14
item.description:
While logged in as bandit14, use nc to send data:

bash
1
nc localhost 30000

Then enter Level 14’s password.

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Level 13
item.description:
The private key is present in the home directory. Use it to login as bandit14 using SSH. The -i flag specifies the private key:

bash
1
ssh -p 2220 bandit14@localhost -i sshkey.private

Note: The permissions of the private key file should be 700.

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Level 12
item.description:
We have the hexdump in ASCII. Use mktemp -d and do all the changes in the tmp folder.

We need to reverse the hexdump using xxd -r:

bash
1
xxd -r filename.txt newfile

Use the file command to get the file signature, then use mv to rename to that extension.

Extension Format
.gz gzip
.tar tar
.bz2 bzip2

To uncompress:

bash
1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8
# gzip
gunzip filename

# tar
tar -x -f archivename

# bzip2
bzip2 filename

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Level 11
item.description:
To rotate the characters use the tool tr. To rotate by 13 positions:

bash
1
cat file_name | tr 'a-z' 'n-za-m' | tr 'A-Z' 'N-ZA-M'

The first argument takes the input string format, the second takes the output format — if a is rotated by 13 positions it becomes n and goes till z, then starts over from a to m. The second pipe is for uppercase characters.

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Level 10
item.description:
Use base64 -d to decode the string:

bash
1
cat data.txt | base64 -d

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Level 9
item.description:
Use strings to print human readable content from a file and pass it to grep with "==" (2 equals signs since “a few” is more than 1):

bash
1
strings data.txt | grep "=="

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Level 8
item.description:
Use sort to sort the words in dictionary order so that repeated words will be together.
Next use uniq to see the unique lines and count the number of words.
uniq -u will directly give the unique word in the file, or you can display the count and grep 1 from there.

Simplest:

bash
1
cat data.txt | sort -f -d | uniq -u

-f is to ignore the case of the letter and -d is for dictionary ordering.

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Level 7
item.description:
Just cat the file and pass it to grep with the word millionth:

bash
1
cat data.txt | grep "millionth"

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Level 6
item.description:
Basic: Go to root directory and find the entire directory with -group, -user and -size, pipe it to file and pipe it to grep to ASCII text.

Full command:

bash
1
find . -group bandit6 -user bandit7 -size 33c -type f | xargs file * | grep "ASCII"

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Level 5
item.description:
Basic: Find the entire directory with size parameter set to 1033 bytes, then pipe it to file which checks for dashed file using -- and then pipe it to grep for ASCII text.

It works only with the file size that is find . -size 1033c (bytes is represented as c).

Full command:

bash
1
find . -size 1033c | xargs file -- * | grep "ASCII"

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Level 4
item.description:
Basic: cat the entire directory because only one file is human readable.

To find which file has ASCII text in the entire directory: find all files and pipe it to file.
We have to use xargs before file. This will give the info about the files, then we can use grep "ASCII" to filter out human readable files.

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Level 3
item.description: Use ls -la to show all files including hidden ones.

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Level 2
item.description: For spaces in the filename, enclose it with quotes.

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Level 1
item.description:
To display contents of a dashed file:

bash
1 2 3
cat < -
# or
cat ./-

Extra: To create a dashed file: touch ./-

item.title: OverTheWire: Bandit — Full Walkthrough
item.description:
Introduction

This is my full collection of OverTheWire Bandit walkthroughs. Each level is covered on its own page with the commands and explanation used to solve it.

Levels

  • Level 1 — Reading a dashed file
  • Level 2 — Spaces in filenames
  • Level 3 — Hidden files with ls -la
  • Level 4 — Finding human-readable files
  • Level 5 — Find file by size
  • Level 6 — Find file by user and group
  • Level 7 — Searching with grep
  • Level 8 — Unique lines with sort and uniq
  • Level 9 — Extracting strings from binary files
  • Level 10 — Base64 decoding
  • Level 11 — ROT13 with the tr command
  • Level 12 — Hexdumps and decompression
  • Level 13 — SSH login with a private key
  • Level 14 — Netcat basics
  • Level 15 — OpenSSL and encrypted connections
  • Level 16 — Port scanning with Nmap
  • Level 17 — Comparing files with diff
  • Level 18 — SSH remote command execution
  • Level 19 — SUID binary and Linux permissions
  • Level 20 — Netcat listeners and SUID binary
  • Level 21 — Cron jobs
  • Level 22 — Cron jobs and bash scripting
  • Level 23 — Cron jobs and bash scripting (continued)
  • Level 24 — Brute forcing with bash and netcat
  • Level 25 & 26 — Escaping restricted environments with more and vim
  • Level 27 — Git — cloning a repository
  • Level 28 — Git — log and checkout
  • Level 29 — Git — branching
  • Level 30 — Git — tags
  • Level 31 — Git — push, commit, and .gitignore
  • Level 32 — Environment variables and shell escape
  • Level 33 — All levels complete!
